Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of AI for Health – Guidance on Large Multi‑Modal Models
Summary: World Health Organization guidance focusing on LMMs in health use cases, covering safety, effectiveness, privacy, bias, accountability, and workforce impacts. Recommends governance measures, evaluation, and monitoring tailored to generative AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals piloting GenAI (e.g., documentation assistants, patient‑facing chat) should align with LMM‑specific safeguards, including transparent labeling, guardrails, and post‑deployment monitoring.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Information for Practitioners Who Provide Virtual Care
Issuing Body: Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency & National Boards
Summary: Updated guidance clarifying that prescribing requires a real‑time consultation (face‑to‑face, video or phone). Asynchronous chats, forms or emails are not a consultation. Flags specific risks when using AI or chatbots and sets expectations for safe virtual care.
Healthcare Implications: Clinicians and telehealth vendors should avoid ‘tick‑box’ prescribing and ensure patient‑clinician interaction before prescribing. Update triage/bot flows, consent, and documentation to reflect that AI/chat is not a consultation for prescribing.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Action Plan for the Use of AI in Operations at the PMDA
Summary: Japan's Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency sets a phased plan to use AI in its internal operations. The plan adopts existing AI tools for search, summarization, minute‑taking, and translation, and pilots proprietary models in a secure environment for specialized review and safety tasks. It also establishes agency‑wide AI governance.
Healthcare Implications: Regulatory reviews, safety assessments, and inspections in Japan may become faster and more consistent as PMDA verifies these tools. Sponsors should anticipate AI‑assisted workflows during interactions and plan submissions with clearer documentation, traceability, and post‑market evidence.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Title: Law No. 132 of 23 Sept 2025 – Provisions and Delegations on Artificial Intelligence
Issuing Body: Parliament of Italy & Presidency of the Republic
Summary: First comprehensive Italian AI law. Sets national AI governance, transparency/labeling duties, enforcement framework; introduces measures addressing deepfakes and sectoral guidance mandates.
Healthcare Implications: Cross-sector law that will govern AI used by public hospitals and health providers (procurement, transparency, risk controls) and AI developers supplying health tools in Italy.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: MOH Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Guidelines (AIHGle)
Summary: Singapore’s sector guidelines (MOH with HSA and Synapxe) outline good practice for healthcare AI across governance, data quality, validation, transparency, workflow integration, monitoring, and alignment with device regulation.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals can adopt the guidance for governance charters, procurement criteria, pre‑deployment validation gates, and post‑deployment monitoring; vendors align documentation and change‑control to support evaluation and safe clinical use.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Artificial intelligence explained – Medical devices
Summary: The Therapeutic Goods Administration's overview of how AI and software are regulated as medical devices, including when AI is considered a device, evidence expectations, and current initiatives on GenAI/LMMs.
Healthcare Implications: Sets expectations for sponsors regarding classification, evidence, and compliance in Australia. Hospitals can use this to understand which AI tools fall under the Therapeutic Goods Administration regulation and what assurances to expect.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Regulation of Software, Including AI – SaMD Rules and Guidance
Summary: Detailed guidance on how the the Therapeutic Goods Administration regulates software-based medical devices, including SaMD definitions, classification, evidence, and obligations across the lifecycle. Recently updated to reflect AI considerations.
Healthcare Implications: Helps developers and providers determine when AI features trigger device regulation and what documentation is required. Supports procurement due diligence and clinical governance for AI-enabled software.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: MDCG 2025-6: Interplay Between MDR/IVDR and the AI Act
Summary: Explains how the AI Act’s duties apply alongside MDR/IVDR for AI-enabled medical devices and IVDs; clarifies conformity assessment, roles, and timing.
Healthcare Implications: Crucial for CE-marking AI medical devices: confirms dual compliance (device + AI Act), affecting manufacturers, notified bodies, and EU health providers procuring AI tools.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Title: MDCG 2025-4 – Guidance on the safe making available of medical device software apps on online platforms
Summary: Medical Device Coordination Group guidance on making medical‑device software apps available through online platforms, explaining when platforms act as distributors and associated duties for CE/UDI verification, traceability, and incident communications.
Healthcare Implications: Developers must ensure compliant listing, labeling, vigilance routes, and rapid takedown; platforms clarify responsibilities; hospitals can update allow‑lists and procurement checks for AI‑enabled health apps reaching clinicians or patients.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: MDCG 2019-11 – Guidance on Qualification and Classification of Software under MDR/IVDR
Summary: Updated Medical Device Coordination Group guidance on when software, including AI/ML functions, qualifies as a medical device and how to classify it under Medical Device Recognition/In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation. Clarifies decision steps, borderline cases, and alignment with other MDCG docs; adds examples and notes on updates/versions.
Healthcare Implications: Essential reference for determining if clinical AI is Software as a Medical Device and for assigning class, which governs evidence and oversight. Product and regulatory teams should map features against the decision tree and classification rules to scope clinical evidence and Post-Market Surveillance/Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF).
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Data and AI in Medicines Regulation to 2028
Summary: EU medicines regulators’ plan to leverage data & AI in regulatory science (governance, LLM use, evidence generation, data interoperability) through 2028.
Healthcare Implications: Shapes how AI tools/data are used in medicines evaluation and pharmacovigilance – affecting sponsors, MAHs, and clinical research that use AI.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Guidance on the Use of AI-Enabled Ambient Scribing Products in Health and Care Settings
Summary: National guidance for deploying AI/GenAI ambient scribing (AVT) tools in clinical settings. This policy covers procurement, safety, evaluation, and information governance, with practical steps for local implementation.
Healthcare Implications: Directly shapes provider deployment of AI scribes (documentation quality, safety risk controls, IG/privacy, workforce impact) and informs vendor requirements for NHS adoption.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Regulation 2025/327 – European Health Data Space
Summary: Creates the European Health Data Space: Electronic Health Record access/portability, common interoperability specs, and a harmonized secondary‑use regime via Health Data Access Body and secure processing across HealthData@EU.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals and vendors face new Electronic Health Record/data duties; researchers and AI developers can request cross‑border datasets for training/validation, analyzed in secure environments under permits, fees, and re‑identification safeguards.
Keyword Tags: Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Policy Name/Title: Guidance on Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices
Summary: Final guidance defining machine learning-enabled medical device scope and expectations for evidence, change management (including predetermined change control plans), and lifecycle oversight in pre‑market submissions. Aligns terminology to IMDRF N67 and encourages robust performance evaluation and real‑world monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Canadian sponsors and partners should structure submissions and validation to meet ML‑specific requirements; healthcare providers adopting machine learning-enabled medical devices can expect clearer labeling and post‑market commitments.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Digital Mental Health Technology: Qualification and Classification
Summary: Clarifies when digital mental health technologies (incl. AI chatbots/decision support) qualify as medical devices and how they are classified and evaluated.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects AI mental-health apps used in care pathways: determines device status, evidence expectations and regulatory route before deployment in the NHS.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development – Guiding Principles (IMDRF/AIML WG/N88 FINAL:2025)
Summary: International guiding principles for Good Machine Learning Practice across the medical device lifecycle, developed by the International Medical Device Regulators Forum's AIML Working Group.
Healthcare Implications: Device developers and hospital innovators can align development, validation, and change management of AI‑enabled devices with these principles; supports harmonized evidence expectations and post‑market practices.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Guiding Principles for Conducting Clinical Trial for Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices
Summary: Joint Health Sciences Authority-Ministry of Food and Drug Safety principles for designing and conducting clinical trials of ML‑enabled medical devices. Covers study design, dataset considerations, performance endpoints, drift/updates during trials, and transparency of algorithms.
Healthcare Implications: Provides practical expectations for sponsors and investigators running machine learning-enabled medical device trials in Asia. Hospitals participating in such trials can align protocols, monitoring, and change‑management to the guidance.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Reflection paper – Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Medicinal Product Lifecycle
Summary: European Medicines Agency reflection paper setting expectations for use of AI/ML across the medicines lifecycle – R&D, clinical trials, manufacturing, and post‑authorisation. Addresses governance, data quality, validation, GxP alignment, and human oversight; provides considerations for submissions and inspections.
Healthcare Implications: Sponsors using AI in trial design, data analysis, pharmacovigilance, or manufacturing should plan transparent methods, validation packages, and risk management. Clinicians participating in trials may see AI‑enabled tools subject to additional documentation and controls.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (CETS No. 225)
Summary: First legally binding international treaty on AI addressing human rights, democracy, and rule of law. Sets baseline obligations for parties to ensure AI lifecycle activities respect fundamental rights and provide effective safeguards and remedies.
Healthcare Implications: Health authorities and providers in signatory states should expect cross‑cutting obligations around rights impact assessment, transparency, and accountability in clinical AI. National implementing laws and guidance may flow from the Convention and apply to healthcare delivery and research.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance

Policy Name/Title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 – Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)
Summary: Horizontal EU law establishing risk-based obligations, bans for certain practices, requirements for high‑risk AI (e.g., quality management, data governance, human oversight), transparency duties for specific systems, and market surveillance/enforcement mechanisms.
Healthcare Implications: Many clinical AI products will fall under existing Medical Device Recognition/In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation as medical devices and also under the AI Act as high‑risk AI (Annex II/III cross‑references), driving additional documentation, post‑market monitoring, and transparency. Hospitals and vendors should align conformity assessment, data governance, and human‑oversight controls across MDR/IVDR and the AI Act.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Software and AI as a Medical Device Change Programme – Roadmap
Issuing Body: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
Summary: Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency multi‑work‑package program to update regulation of software and AI as medical devices across the lifecycle, including qualification, pre‑market evidence, post‑market monitoring, and transparency.
Healthcare Implications: Signals evolving UK expectations for AI medical devices. Developers and National Health Service providers should track deliverables that affect submissions, labeling, and post‑market surveillance.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Impact of AI on the Regulation of Medical Products – Policy Paper
Summary: Sets out Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency's approach to medicines/devices regulation in the age of AI, including evidence expectations, adaptivity, international recognition, and collaboration with the International Medical Device Regulators Forum and other regulators.
Healthcare Implications: Informs sponsors and National Health Service partners about the regulator’s priorities, including transparency and post‑market monitoring for AI‑enabled products. Useful for aligning development plans and regulatory strategies.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk

Policy Name/Title: Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (v1.0)
Summary: Singapore’s model governance framework tailored to Generative AI, adding dimensions like content provenance, model evaluation/testing, incident reporting, and accountability while building on the 2019/2020 framework. Includes suggested practices and implementation levers.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for hospitals and vendors deploying GenAI for documentation, triage, and patient engagement. Provides concrete controls (e.g., testing, red‑teaming, transparency) that complement clinical safety and privacy requirements.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance

Policy Name/Title: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: Nonbinding resource describing regulatory considerations for AI in health across the product lifecycle. Addresses data quality, risk management, transparency, post‑market monitoring, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Manufacturers and regulators can use this as a checklist when designing oversight for AI‑enabled health technologies. Hospitals should anticipate documentation needs for procurement and clinical governance.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative AI Services
Summary: The Cyberspace Administration of China‑led measures regulating public‑facing generative AI services, emphasizing content governance, security assessments, data quality, and user rights. Requires providers to take responsibility for generated content and to implement risk controls.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts China‑based health apps and GenAI tools used by patients/clinicians, imposing content and data controls. International vendors operating in China must adapt service configurations and compliance processes.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health (FG‑AI4H DEL02)
Summary: International Telecommunication Union/World Health Organization focus group deliverable summarizing regulatory considerations for AI in health, including ethics, risk management, performance evaluation, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Useful overview for ministries and regulators designing AI oversight and for providers seeking to understand cross‑jurisdictional expectations.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices – Key Terms and Definitions (AIMD WG)
Summary: Defines core terms for ML‑enabled medical devices to promote consistent regulatory language and scope across jurisdictions.
Healthcare Implications: Enables consistent classification, documentation, and evaluation of machine learning-enabled medical devices across regulators and clinical users; often referenced by national guidances (e.g., Health Canada MLMD).
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Title: Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Global ethical standard adopted by 193 Member States covering human rights, inclusion, data governance, education, and environmental sustainability in AI. Encourages national implementation via action plans and monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems can use the Recommendation to shape equitable AI policies, informed consent and transparency practices, and workforce training. Reinforces safeguards for vulnerable populations in clinical AI deployment.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health – WHO Guidance
Summary: World Health Organization guidance outlining ethical challenges and six consensus principles for AI in health, with recommendations for governance, regulation, and rights‑preserving design.
Healthcare Implications: Useful baseline for hospital AI committees and ministries to establish ethics reviews, bias assessment, and transparency practices for AI used in care and public health.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance

Policy Name/Title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
Summary: Government charter committing agencies to transparency, accountability, and oversight in the use of algorithms, including risk assessment and public communication.
Healthcare Implications: Public health agencies in New Zealand using AI/algorithms should meet Charter commitments – useful reference for transparency and engagement with Māori data sovereignty considerations.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance

Policy Name/Title: OECD Recommendation on Artificial Intelligence (OECD/LEGAL/0449)
Summary: First intergovernmental standard on AI adopted by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Council. Sets principles for trustworthy, human‑centred AI and policy recommendations for national strategies, research, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a governance benchmark referenced by many health‑sector policies. Health ministries and systems can map internal AI programs to the Recommendation’s principles to support accountability and cross‑border alignment.
